content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
1102 Q Street • Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

September 12, 2019

Thomas Krouse
Treasurer/Candidate
6637 Curiew Terrace
Carlsbad, CA 92011

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-19-172

Dear Mr. Krouse:

This letter responds to your request for advice regarding campaign provisions of the
Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹

Please note that we are not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) I
FPPC Ops. 71), and any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate. If this is
not the case or if the facts underlying these decisions should change, you should contact us for
additional advice.

QUESTION

May you pay the campaign statement ("Form 460") late filing penalties for all three of your
candidate controlled committees for elective state office out of personal funds directly to the
California Secretary of State (the "Secretary of State") without having to first deposit the funds into
the committee accounts?

CONCLUSION

Yes. The Act does not prohibit payment of late filing penalties imposed upon a candidate
controlled committee for elective state office with a candidate's personal funds. If the late filing
penalties are paid from your personal funds, the funds do not need to first be deposited into the
committee accounts.

FACTS AS PRESENTED BY REQUESTER

You were an unsuccessful candidate for State Assembly in three elections for the years
2014, 2016, and 2018. You have open committees for each of these three elections and serve as the
treasurer for each of the three committees. For each of these committees, campaign statements

¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

File No. A-19-172
Page No. 2

(Form 460) were filed past the deadline, causing an accrual of late filing penalties payable to the
Secretary of State. You would like to pay the late filing penalties directly to the Secretary of State,
without first having to deposit personal funds into the campaign account.

ANALYSIS

The Act requires candidates and committees to file periodic campaign statements disclosing
contributions received and expenditures made in connection with state and local elections. (Section
84100 et seq.) Any person who files a campaign statement or report after the deadline imposed by
the Political Reform Act is liable for a late fine pursuant to Government Code Section 91013.

Section 91013 states:

"(a) If any person files an original statement or report after any deadline imposed
by this act, he or she shall, in addition to any other penalties or remedies
established by this act, be liable in the amount of ten dollars ($10) per day after
the deadline until the statement or report is filed, to the officer with whom the
statement or report is required to be filed. Liability need not be enforced by the
filing officer if on an impartial basis he or she determines that the late filing was
not willful and that enforcement of the liability will not further the purposes of the
act, except that no liability shall be waived if a statement or report is not filed
within 30 days for a statement of economic interest, other than a candidate's
statement filed pursuant to Section 87201, five days for a campaign statement
required to be filed 12 days before an election, and 10 days for all other
statements or reports, after the filing officer has sent specific written notice of the
filing requirement.

"(b) If any person files a copy of a statement or report after any deadline imposed
by this act, he or she shall, in addition to any other penalties or remedies
established by this chapter, be liable in the amount of ten dollars ($10) per day,
starting 10 days, or five days in the case of a campaign statement required to be
filed 12 days before an election, after the officer has sent specific written notice of
the filing requirement and until the statement is filed.

"(c) The officer shall deposit any funds received under this section into the
general fund of the jurisdiction of which he or she is an officer. No liability under
this section shall exceed the cumulative amount stated in the late statement or
report, or one hundred dollars ($100), whichever is greater."

Based on the facts provided, you are the treasurer and candidate of three separate candidate
controlled committees for elective state office that were required to file campaign reports and each
of these committees have incurred late filing penalties in connection with the Form 460. You have
asked whether these late filing penalties may be paid out of your personal funds without having to
first deposit the funds in into the committees' bank accounts. The Act imposes joint and several
liability when two or more persons are liable for any violation of the Act. (Section 91006.)² Along

2 "Person" under the Act includes both individuals and committees. (Section 82047).

File No. A-19-172
Page No. 3

with the committees, you are jointly and severally liable for the late filing penalties. Therefore, the
late filing penalties may be paid with your personal funds directly to the Secretary of State without
first being deposited into the committees' accounts.³

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Dave Bainbridge
General Counsel

[signature]

By: Katelyn Greene
Counsel, Legal Division

KG:sal

³ Payments for fines made with personal funds are not required to be reported on subsequent campaign reports.

sections.question:
May you pay the campaign statement ("Form 460") late filing penalties for all three of your 
candidate controlled committees for elective state office out of personal funds directly to the 
California Secretary of State (the "Secretary of State") without having to first deposit the funds into 
the committee accounts?
sections.conclusion:
Yes. The Act does not prohibit payment of late filing penalties imposed upon a candidate 
controlled committee for elective state office with a candidate's personal funds. If the late filing 
penalties are paid from your personal funds, the funds do not need to first be deposited into the 
committee accounts.

the Political Reform Act is liable for a late fine pursuant to Government Code Section 91013. 
Section 91013 states: 
"(a) If any person files an original statement or report after any deadline imposed

established by this act, be liable in the amount often dollars ($10) per day after 
the deadline until the statement or report is filed, to the officer with whom the 
statement or report is required to be filed. Liability need not be enforced by the 
filing officer if on an impartial basis he or she determines that the late filing was 
not willful and that enforcement of the liability will not further the purposes of the

statement filed pursuant to Section 87201, five days for a campaign statement 
required to be filed 12 days before an election, and 10 days for all other 
statements or reports, after the filing officer has sent specific written notice of the

"(b) If any person files a copy of a statement or report after any deadline imposed 
by this act, he or she shall, in addition to any other penalties or remedies

starting 10 days, or five days in the case of a campaign statement required to be 
filed 12 days before an election, after the officer has sent specific written notice of 
the filing requirement and until the statement is filed. 
"( c) The officer shall deposit any funds received under this section into th,e 
general fund of the jurisdiction of which he or she is an officer. No liability under 
this section shall exceed the cumulative amount stated in the late statement or 
report, or one hundred dollars ($100), whichever is greater." 
Based on the facts provided, you are the treasurer and candidate of three separate candidate

2 "Person" under the Act includes both individuals and committees. (Section 82047).  
with the committees, you are jointly and severally liable for the late filing penalties. Therefore, the 
late filing penalties may be paid with your personal funds directly to the Secretary of State without 
first being deposited into the committees' accounts.3
